guide.title: Complete Python Guide: Basic to Advanced
guide.description: A comprehensive, interview-ready Python guide covering all concepts from fundamentals to advanced internals with code examples, inputs/outputs, and interview Q&A.

topics.title: Introduction to Python
topics.explanation: Python is a high-level, interpreted, dynamically-typed programming language created by Guido van Rossum in 1991. It emphasizes code readability and simplicity, following the Zen of Python philosophy.

subtopics.title: Zen of Python
subtopics.explanation: The guiding principles of Python's design, accessible via `import this`.
subtopics.code: import this
subtopics.output:
The Zen of Python, by Tim Peters
Beautiful is better than ugly.
Explicit is better than implicit.
Simple is better than complex...

subtopics.title: Python 2 vs Python 3
subtopics.explanation: Python 3 (released 2008) is the current standard. Key differences include print as a function, true division, unicode strings by default, and range returning an iterator.
subtopics.code:
# Python 3 specifics
print(type(5/2))   # float division
print(type(5//2))  # integer division
print(type('hello'))  # str is unicode by default

subtopics.title: Running Python
subtopics.explanation: Python can be run interactively (REPL), as scripts, or in notebooks.
subtopics.code:
# Check Python version
import sys
print(sys.version)
print(sys.version_info)
subtopics.output:
3.11.0 (main, Oct 24 2022)
sys.version_info(major=3, minor=11, micro=0, releaselevel='final', serial=0)

interview_qa.question: What is the GIL in Python?
interview_qa.answer: The Global Interpreter Lock (GIL) is a mutex in CPython that allows only one thread to execute Python bytecode at a time. It simplifies memory management but limits multi-threaded CPU-bound performance. Use multiprocessing or async for parallelism.

interview_qa.question: Is Python interpreted or compiled?
interview_qa.answer: Python is both. Source code is compiled to bytecode (.pyc files) which is then interpreted by the CPython virtual machine. This is why Python is often called 'interpreted' but technically it has a compilation step.

topics.title: Variables & Data Types
topics.explanation: Python uses dynamic typing — variables don't have fixed types. Types are inferred at runtime and can change.

subtopics.title: Basic Types
subtopics.explanation: Python's built-in scalar types: int, float, complex, str, bool, None.
subtopics.code:
x = 42           # int
y = 3.14         # float
z = 2 + 3j       # complex
s = 'hello'      # str
b = True         # bool
n = None         # NoneType

print(type(x), type(y), type(z))
print(type(s), type(b), type(n))

subtopics.title: Dynamic Typing
subtopics.explanation: Variables can be reassigned to different types. Use type() and isinstance() to check types.
subtopics.code:
x = 10
print(type(x))   # int
x = 'hello'
print(type(x))   # str now

print(isinstance(42, int))       # True
print(isinstance(42, (int, float)))  # True - checks multiple

subtopics.title: Type Casting
subtopics.explanation: Convert between types explicitly using constructor functions.
subtopics.code:
print(int('42'))        # str -> int
print(float('3.14'))    # str -> float
print(str(100))         # int -> str
print(bool(0))          # 0 -> False
print(bool(1))          # 1 -> True
print(bool(''))         # empty str -> False
print(bool('hello'))    # non-empty -> True
print(int(3.9))         # truncates, not rounds -> 3
print(list('abc'))      # str -> list of chars

subtopics.title: Mutable vs Immutable
subtopics.explanation: Immutable types: int, float, str, tuple, frozenset, bytes. Mutable types: list, dict, set. Immutability affects assignment behavior and hashability.
subtopics.code:
# Immutable - reassignment creates new object
a = 'hello'
b = a
a = a + ' world'  # new string object
print(b)          # b unchanged

# Mutable - both point to same object
list1 = [1, 2, 3]
list2 = list1
list1.append(4)
print(list2)      # list2 also changed!

interview_qa.question: What is the difference between mutable and immutable types?
interview_qa.answer: Mutable objects (list, dict, set) can be changed after creation. Immutable objects (int, str, tuple, frozenset) cannot. Immutability makes objects hashable (usable as dict keys). When you 'modify' an immutable, Python creates a new object.

interview_qa.question: What does `is` vs `==` check?
interview_qa.answer: `==` checks value equality. `is` checks identity (same object in memory via id()). Example: `a = [1,2]; b = [1,2]; a == b` is True but `a is b` is False. Small integers (-5 to 256) and interned strings may share identity due to caching.

topics.title: Operators
topics.explanation: Python supports arithmetic, comparison, logical, bitwise, assignment, identity, and membership operators.

subtopics.title: Arithmetic Operators
subtopics.explanation: Basic math operations including floor division, modulo, and exponentiation.
subtopics.code:
print(10 + 3)   # 13  addition
print(10 - 3)   # 7   subtraction
print(10 * 3)   # 30  multiplication
print(10 / 3)   # 3.333... true division
print(10 // 3)  # 3   floor division
print(10 % 3)   # 1   modulo (remainder)
print(2 ** 10)  # 1024 exponentiation
print(-7 // 2)  # -4  floor toward negative infinity

subtopics.title: Comparison Operators
subtopics.explanation: Return boolean values. Python supports chained comparisons.
subtopics.code:
print(5 == 5)    # True
print(5 != 3)    # True
print(5 > 3)     # True
print(5 < 3)     # False
print(5 >= 5)    # True
print(5 <= 4)    # False

# Chained comparison (Pythonic!)
x = 5
print(1 < x < 10)   # True
print(1 < x < 4)    # False
subtopics.output:
True
True
True
False
True
False
True
False

subtopics.title: Logical Operators
subtopics.explanation: and, or, not with short-circuit evaluation. Return the actual values, not just True/False.
subtopics.code:
# Short-circuit: returns first falsy for 'and', first truthy for 'or'
print(True and False)   # False
print(True or False)    # True
print(not True)         # False

# Returns actual values (useful pattern)
print(0 or 'default')   # 'default' (0 is falsy)
print(5 or 'default')   # 5 (5 is truthy)
print(None and 'never') # None (short-circuits)
print([] or {} or 42)   # 42 (first truthy)

subtopics.title: Bitwise Operators
subtopics.explanation: Operate on integer bits directly.
subtopics.code:
a, b = 0b1010, 0b1100  # 10 and 12
print(bin(a & b))   # AND:  0b1000 = 8
print(bin(a | b))   # OR:   0b1110 = 14
print(bin(a ^ b))   # XOR:  0b0110 = 6
print(bin(~a))      # NOT:  -11 (two's complement)
print(bin(a << 1))  # Left shift:  0b10100 = 20
print(bin(a >> 1))  # Right shift: 0b0101 = 5

subtopics.title: Identity & Membership
subtopics.explanation: is/is not for object identity; in/not in for membership testing.
subtopics.code:
# Identity
a = [1, 2]
b = a
c = [1, 2]
print(a is b)    # True - same object
print(a is c)    # False - different objects
print(a == c)    # True - equal values

# Membership
print(3 in [1, 2, 3])        # True
print('py' in 'python')      # True
print('key' in {'key': 1})   # True (checks keys)
print(5 not in range(3))     # True
subtopics.output:
True
False
True
True
True
True
True

interview_qa.question: What is operator precedence in Python?
interview_qa.answer: From highest to lowest: ** (exponent), ~+- (unary), */%// , +-, <<>>, &, ^, |, comparisons, not, and, or. Use parentheses to make intent explicit.

interview_qa.question: How does Python's `or` operator work for default values?
interview_qa.answer: `x = value or default` returns `value` if truthy, else `default`. But beware: if value is 0, '', [], etc. (valid but falsy), it returns default unexpectedly. Prefer `x = value if value is not None else default` for precision.

topics.title: Strings — All Methods
topics.explanation: Strings in Python are immutable sequences of Unicode characters. They have a rich set of methods for manipulation.

subtopics.title: String Creation & Slicing
subtopics.explanation: Strings can be created with single, double, or triple quotes. Slicing uses [start:stop:step].
subtopics.code:
s = 'Hello, World!'
print(s[0])       # H
print(s[-1])      # !
print(s[0:5])     # Hello
print(s[7:])      # World!
print(s[:5])      # Hello
print(s[::2])     # Hlo ol!
print(s[::-1])    # !dlroW ,olleH (reversed)
subtopics.output:
H
!
Hello
World!
Hello
Hlo ol!
!dlroW ,olleH

subtopics.title: Case Methods
subtopics.explanation: Methods for changing string case.
subtopics.code:
s = 'hello WORLD'
print(s.upper())        # HELLO WORLD
print(s.lower())        # hello world
print(s.capitalize())   # Hello world (first char upper)
print(s.title())        # Hello World (each word)
print(s.swapcase())     # HELLO world
subtopics.output:
HELLO WORLD
hello world
Hello world
Hello World
HELLO world

subtopics.title: Search Methods
subtopics.explanation: find returns -1 if not found; index raises ValueError.
subtopics.code:
s = 'hello world hello'
print(s.find('hello'))          # 0 (first occurrence)
print(s.find('hello', 1))       # 12 (search from index 1)
print(s.rfind('hello'))         # 12 (from right)
print(s.find('xyz'))            # -1 (not found)
# s.index('xyz')               # raises ValueError
print(s.count('hello'))         # 2
print(s.startswith('hello'))    # True
print(s.endswith('hello'))      # True
print(s.startswith('world', 6)) # True (from index 6)

subtopics.title: Check Methods (is*)
subtopics.explanation: Boolean check methods for string content.
subtopics.code:
print('abc'.isalpha())      # True - all letters
print('123'.isdigit())      # True - all digits
print('abc123'.isalnum())   # True - letters or digits
print('   '.isspace())      # True - all whitespace
print('hello'.islower())    # True
print('HELLO'.isupper())    # True
print('Hello World'.istitle()) # True
print('var_1'.isidentifier())  # True - valid Python name
print('123'.isdecimal())    # True
print('123'.isnumeric())    # True (also handles fractions)
print('abc'.isprintable())  # True
subtopics.output:
True
True
True
True
True
True
True
True
True
True
True

subtopics.title: Modify Methods
subtopics.explanation: Strip whitespace/chars, replace substrings.
subtopics.code:
s = '  hello world  '
print(repr(s.strip()))        # 'hello world'
print(repr(s.lstrip()))       # 'hello world  '
print(repr(s.rstrip()))       # '  hello world'
print('***hi***'.strip('*'))  # hi

print('hello'.replace('l', 'r'))      # herro
print('hello'.replace('l', 'r', 1))   # herlo (max 1 replace)

# Python 3.9+
print('hello world'.removeprefix('hello '))  # world
print('hello world'.removesuffix(' world'))  # hello
subtopics.output:
'hello world'
'hello world  '
'  hello world'
hi
herro
herlo
world
hello

subtopics.title: Split & Join
subtopics.explanation: Split strings into lists; join lists into strings.
subtopics.code:
s = 'a,b,c,d'
print(s.split(','))          # ['a', 'b', 'c', 'd']
print(s.split(',', 2))       # ['a', 'b', 'c,d'] (max 2 splits)
print(s.rsplit(',', 1))      # ['a,b,c', 'd']
print('hello\nworld'.splitlines())  # ['hello', 'world']

# join - reverse of split
words = ['Hello', 'World']
print(' '.join(words))   # Hello World
print('-'.join('abc'))   # a-b-c

subtopics.title: Align & Pad Methods
subtopics.explanation: Align text within a field width.
subtopics.code:
print('hi'.center(10))        #     hi    
print('hi'.center(10, '-'))   # ----hi----
print('hi'.ljust(10, '.'))    # hi........
print('hi'.rjust(10, '.'))    # ........hi
print('42'.zfill(6))          # 000042
print('-42'.zfill(6))         # -00042

subtopics.title: Format Methods
subtopics.explanation: Multiple ways to format strings in Python.
subtopics.code:
name, age = 'Alice', 30

# f-strings (Python 3.6+) - preferred
print(f'Name: {name}, Age: {age}')
print(f'{3.14159:.2f}')   # 2 decimal places
print(f'{1000000:,}')     # comma separator
print(f'{42:08b}')        # binary, zero-padded
print(f'{name!r}')        # repr
print(f'{age=}')          # debug: prints age=30

# .format()
print('{} is {}'.format(name, age))
print('{0} and {0}'.format('echo'))
print('{n} is {a}'.format(n=name, a=age))
subtopics.output:
Name: Alice, Age: 30
3.14
1,000,000
00101010
'Alice'
age=30
Alice is 30
echo and echo
Alice is 30

subtopics.title: Partition & Translate
subtopics.explanation: partition splits into exactly 3 parts; translate maps characters.
subtopics.code:
# partition
print('hello:world'.partition(':'))   # ('hello', ':', 'world')
print('hello world'.partition(':'))   # ('hello world', '', '')
print('a:b:c'.rpartition(':'))        # ('a:b', ':', 'c')

# translate + maketrans
table = str.maketrans('aeiou', '12345')
print('hello world'.translate(table))  # h2ll4 w4rld

# Delete chars with translate
table2 = str.maketrans('', '', 'aeiou')  # delete vowels
print('hello world'.translate(table2))   # hll wrld

subtopics.title: Encode/Decode
subtopics.explanation: Convert between str and bytes.
subtopics.code:
s = 'hello'
b = s.encode('utf-8')      # str -> bytes
print(b)                   # b'hello'
print(type(b))             # bytes

decoded = b.decode('utf-8')  # bytes -> str
print(decoded)               # hello

# Unicode example
u = 'caf\u00e9'  # café
print(u.encode('utf-8'))   # b'caf\xc3\xa9'

interview_qa.question: Why is string concatenation in a loop bad? What's the alternative?
interview_qa.answer: Strings are immutable, so each `s += x` creates a new string object — O(n²) total. Use `''.join(list_of_strings)` which is O(n). Example: `result = ''.join(str(i) for i in range(1000))`.

interview_qa.question: What's the difference between find() and index()?
interview_qa.answer: `find()` returns -1 if substring not found. `index()` raises a `ValueError`. Use `find()` when absence is a valid case; use `index()` when absence means a bug.

interview_qa.question: How do f-strings differ from .format()?
interview_qa.answer: f-strings (3.6+) are evaluated at runtime and generally faster. They support expressions inline: `f'{2+2}'`. `.format()` is more flexible for templates stored as strings since f-strings must be defined inline. f-strings also support `=` for debugging: `f'{var=}'`.

subtopics.title: if / elif / else
subtopics.explanation: Basic branching. Python uses indentation for blocks.

subtopics.title: Ternary Expression
subtopics.explanation: Inline conditional: value_if_true if condition else value_if_false
subtopics.code:
age = 20
status = 'adult' if age >= 18 else 'minor'
print(status)

# Nested ternary (avoid for readability)
grade = 85
result = 'A' if grade >= 90 else 'B' if grade >= 80 else 'C'
print(result)

subtopics.title: match / case (Python 3.10+)
subtopics.explanation: Structural pattern matching — more powerful than switch/case in other languages.
subtopics.code:
command = 'quit'
match command:
    case 'quit':
        print('Quitting')
    case 'start':
        print('Starting')
    case _:
        print('Unknown command')

# Match with destructuring
point = (1, 0)
match point:
    case (0, 0):
        print('Origin')
    case (x, 0):
        print(f'On x-axis at {x}')
    case (0, y):
        print(f'On y-axis at {y}')
    case (x, y):
        print(f'Point at ({x},{y})')

subtopics.title: Truthy and Falsy Values
subtopics.explanation: In Python, many objects are implicitly False in boolean context.
subtopics.code:
# Falsy values
falsy = [False, 0, 0.0, '', [], {}, set(), None, 0j]
for val in falsy:
    print(f'{repr(val):10} -> {bool(val)}')

# Truthy: everything else
print(bool(42))     # True
print(bool([0]))    # True - non-empty list
subtopics.output:
False      -> False
0          -> False
0.0        -> False
''         -> False
[]         -> False
{}         -> False
set()      -> False
None       -> False
0j         -> False
True
True

interview_qa.question: What is the difference between `if x:` and `if x is not None:`?
interview_qa.answer: `if x:` is False for None, 0, '', [], {} etc. `if x is not None:` is only False for None. Use the latter when 0 or empty string is a valid value you want to keep.

subtopics.title: for loop
subtopics.explanation: Iterates over any iterable. The most Pythonic looping construct.
subtopics.code:
# Basic for loop
for i in range(5):
    print(i, end=' ')
print()

# Loop with enumerate
fruits = ['apple', 'banana', 'cherry']
for i, fruit in enumerate(fruits):
    print(f'{i}: {fruit}')

# Loop with zip
names = ['Alice', 'Bob']
scores = [90, 85]
for name, score in zip(names, scores):
    print(f'{name}: {score}')

subtopics.title: while loop
subtopics.explanation: Repeats while condition is True. Use when iteration count is unknown.
subtopics.code:
# Basic while
n = 1
while n < 32:
    n *= 2
print(n)  # 32

# while with break
import random
random.seed(42)
while True:
    val = random.randint(1, 10)
    if val == 5:
        print(f'Found 5!')
        break
    print(val, end=' ')

subtopics.title: break, continue, pass
subtopics.explanation: break exits loop; continue skips to next iteration; pass is a no-op placeholder.

subtopics.title: Iterating with enumerate, zip, reversed
subtopics.explanation: Built-in helpers for common iteration patterns.
subtopics.code:
# enumerate with start index
for i, v in enumerate(['a','b','c'], start=1):
    print(i, v)

# zip stops at shortest
for a, b in zip([1,2,3], ['x','y']):
    print(a, b)

# reversed
for x in reversed([1,2,3,4]):
    print(x, end=' ')
print()

# zip_longest fills missing values
from itertools import zip_longest
for a, b in zip_longest([1,2,3], ['x'], fillvalue='?'):
    print(a, b)

interview_qa.question: What is the for-else construct in Python?
interview_qa.answer: The `else` block of a `for` or `while` loop runs only if the loop completes without hitting a `break`. Useful for search algorithms: loop to find item, break if found, else block handles 'not found' case.

interview_qa.question: What's the difference between range() and list(range())?
interview_qa.answer: `range()` returns a lazy range object (O(1) memory regardless of size). `list(range(n))` materializes it into a list (O(n) memory). Always prefer `range()` in for loops.

subtopics.title: List Comprehensions
subtopics.explanation: Concise way to create lists. [expression for item in iterable if condition]
subtopics.code:
# Basic
squares = [x**2 for x in range(10)]
print(squares)

# With condition
evens = [x for x in range(20) if x % 2 == 0]
print(evens)

# Nested
matrix = [[i*j for j in range(1,4)] for i in range(1,4)]
for row in matrix:
    print(row)

# Flatten nested list
nested = [[1,2],[3,4],[5,6]]
flat = [x for sub in nested for x in sub]
print(flat)

subtopics.title: Dict & Set Comprehensions
subtopics.explanation: Comprehension syntax for dictionaries and sets.

subtopics.title: Generator Expressions
subtopics.explanation: Like list comprehensions but lazy — values computed on demand. Use () instead of [].
subtopics.code:
# Generator expression - memory efficient
gen = (x**2 for x in range(1000000))
print(type(gen))      # generator object
print(next(gen))      # 0
print(next(gen))      # 1

# sum of squares without creating full list
total = sum(x**2 for x in range(1000))
print(total)

# Memory comparison
import sys
list_comp = [x**2 for x in range(1000)]
gen_exp   = (x**2 for x in range(1000))
print(f'List: {sys.getsizeof(list_comp)} bytes')
print(f'Gen:  {sys.getsizeof(gen_exp)} bytes')

interview_qa.question: When should you NOT use list comprehensions?
interview_qa.answer: Avoid when: (1) logic is complex — use a regular loop for readability, (2) you need side effects like printing (use a loop), (3) you only need to iterate once — use a generator expression instead to save memory.

interview_qa.question: What is the difference between a generator expression and a list comprehension?
interview_qa.answer: List comprehension creates the full list in memory immediately. Generator expression is lazy — yields one item at a time. Use generators for large datasets or infinite sequences. Generators are also faster when you don't need all items (e.g., using `any()` or `next()`).

topics.title: Lists — All Methods
topics.explanation: Lists are ordered, mutable sequences. They can contain mixed types.

subtopics.title: All List Methods
subtopics.explanation: Complete reference for all list methods with examples.
subtopics.code:
lst = [3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9]

# append - add to end
lst.append(2)
print(lst)       # [3,1,4,1,5,9,2]

# extend - merge iterable
lst.extend([6, 5])
print(lst)       # [3,1,4,1,5,9,2,6,5]

# insert(index, value)
lst.insert(0, 0)
print(lst)       # [0,3,1,4,1,5,9,2,6,5]

# remove - removes first occurrence
lst.remove(1)
print(lst)       # [0,3,4,1,5,9,2,6,5]

# pop - removes and returns by index (default: last)
val = lst.pop()
print(val, lst)  # 5, [0,3,4,1,5,9,2,6]

# index(x, start, end) - find position
lst2 = [10, 20, 30, 20, 40]
print(lst2.index(20))      # 1
print(lst2.index(20, 2))   # 3 (search from index 2)

# count
print([1,1,2,3,1].count(1))  # 3

# sort - in-place, stable
nums = [3,1,4,1,5,9]
nums.sort()
print(nums)  # [1,1,3,4,5,9]
nums.sort(reverse=True)
print(nums)  # [9,5,4,3,1,1]
nums.sort(key=lambda x: -x)  # same as reverse
print(nums)

# reverse - in-place
nums.reverse()
print(nums)

# copy - shallow copy
copy = nums.copy()

# clear
copy.clear()
print(copy)   # []

subtopics.title: sorted() vs sort()
subtopics.explanation: sorted() returns new list; sort() modifies in-place.
subtopics.code:
nums = [3, 1, 4, 1, 5]
result = sorted(nums)          # new list
print(nums)                    # original unchanged
print(result)

# Sort strings by length
words = ['banana', 'apple', 'fig', 'cherry']
print(sorted(words, key=len))

# Sort by multiple criteria
people = [('Alice', 30), ('Bob', 25), ('Charlie', 30)]
print(sorted(people, key=lambda x: (x[1], x[0])))

subtopics.title: Shallow vs Deep Copy
subtopics.explanation: Shallow copy copies the list but not nested objects. Deep copy copies everything.
subtopics.code:
import copy

# Shallow copy - nested objects still shared
original = [[1, 2], [3, 4]]
shallow = original.copy()  # or list(original) or original[:]
shallow[0].append(99)
print(original)  # [[1,2,99],[3,4]] - inner list affected!

# Deep copy - fully independent
original2 = [[1, 2], [3, 4]]
deep = copy.deepcopy(original2)
deep[0].append(99)
print(original2)  # [[1,2],[3,4]] - unchanged

interview_qa.question: What is the time complexity of list operations?
interview_qa.answer: append: O(1) amortized, pop from end: O(1), pop from index: O(n), insert at index: O(n), index lookup by index: O(1), search by value: O(n), sort: O(n log n).

interview_qa.question: How is list.sort() different from sorted()?
interview_qa.answer: list.sort() modifies the list in-place and returns None. sorted() returns a new list and works on any iterable. Both accept key and reverse parameters. list.sort() is slightly faster since it doesn't create a new list.

interview_qa.question: Why use tuples instead of lists?
interview_qa.answer: Tuples: (1) are immutable so hashable — usable as dict keys or set elements, (2) slightly faster than lists due to fixed size, (3) signal intent that data shouldn't change (e.g., coordinates, RGB values), (4) can be used for multiple return values.

interview_qa.question: What is the time complexity of dict operations?
interview_qa.answer: Average O(1) for get, set, delete, and `in` operator. Worst case O(n) due to hash collisions (rare). Iteration is O(n).

interview_qa.question: How would you count word frequency in a sentence?
interview_qa.answer: Use Counter: `from collections import Counter; freq = Counter(sentence.split())`. Or manually: `freq = {}; freq[word] = freq.get(word, 0) + 1`.

interview_qa.question: How would you remove duplicates from a list while preserving order?
interview_qa.answer: Python 3.7+: `list(dict.fromkeys(lst))` — dicts preserve insertion order. Or: `seen = set(); result = [x for x in lst if not (x in seen or seen.add(x))]`. Simple `list(set(lst))` removes duplicates but loses order.

interview_qa.question: What is the mutable default argument trap?
interview_qa.answer: Default arguments are evaluated ONCE at function definition, not each call. `def f(lst=[]):` shares the same list across calls. Fix: `def f(lst=None): if lst is None: lst = []`.

subtopics.title: LEGB Rule
subtopics.explanation: Python looks up names in: Local -> Enclosing -> Global -> Built-in.
subtopics.code:
x = 'global'

def outer():
    x = 'enclosing'
    def inner():
        x = 'local'
        print(x)    # local
    inner()
    print(x)        # enclosing

outer()
print(x)            # global

# global keyword
count = 0
def increment():
    global count
    count += 1
increment()
print(count)   # 1

# nonlocal keyword
def make_counter():
    n = 0
    def counter():
        nonlocal n
        n += 1
        return n
    return counter

c = make_counter()
print(c(), c(), c())

def multiplier(factor):
    def multiply(x):
        return x * factor  # 'factor' is closed over
    return multiply

double = multiplier(2)
triple = multiplier(3)
print(double(5))   # 10
print(triple(5))   # 15

# Inspect closure
print(double.__closure__[0].cell_contents)  # 2

    return lambda x: x + n
add5 = make_adder(5)
print(add5(10))  # 15

interview_qa.question: Explain the closure variable capture gotcha in loops.
interview_qa.answer: Functions in a loop capture the variable, not its value: `funcs = [lambda: i for i in range(3)]` — all return 2 (last value). Fix: use default arg: `lambda i=i: i` or `functools.partial`.

interview_qa.question: Why use @functools.wraps in decorators?
interview_qa.answer: Without it, the wrapper function replaces the original, losing __name__, __doc__, __module__, etc. This breaks introspection, documentation tools, and debugging. @wraps copies these attributes from the wrapped function to the wrapper.

interview_qa.question: What is the difference between a decorator and a context manager?
interview_qa.answer: Decorators modify or wrap a function's behavior permanently. Context managers manage resources for a block of code using with statement — they guarantee setup/teardown (like file closing). Both can be implemented similarly but serve different use cases.

subtopics.title: yield and Generator Functions
subtopics.explanation: A function with yield is a generator — returns values lazily.
subtopics.code:
def countdown(n):
    while n > 0:
        yield n
        n -= 1

gen = countdown(5)
print(next(gen))  # 5
print(next(gen))  # 4
for val in gen:   # continues from 3
    print(val, end=' ')
print()

# Infinite generator
def fibonacci():
    a, b = 0, 1
    while True:
        yield a
        a, b = b, a + b

fib = fibonacci()
print([next(fib) for _ in range(10)])

interview_qa.question: What is the difference between yield and return?
interview_qa.answer: return exits the function completely. yield pauses execution, returns a value, and resumes from the same point on the next call to next(). A function with yield is a generator — calling it returns a generator object, not the result.

interview_qa.question: How would you implement an infinite sequence in Python?
interview_qa.answer: Use a generator: `def naturals(): n=0; while True: yield n; n+=1`. Generators are lazy so they don't compute all values. Combine with itertools.islice to take a finite portion.

class Employee:

    def __init__(self, name):

emp1 = Employee('Alice')
emp2 = Employee('Bob')

print(emp2.company)  # Data Cake

subtopics.title: Methods: Instance, Class, and Static
subtopics.explanation: 1. Instance Methods: Take `self`. Access instance data. 2. Class Methods: Take `cls`. Access class-level data. 3. Static Methods: Take nothing. Pure utility functions.

interview_qa.question: What is the difference between @classmethod, @staticmethod, and instance method?
interview_qa.answer: Instance method receives `self` (instance). @classmethod receives `cls` (class) — used for factory methods, alternative constructors. @staticmethod receives nothing special — it's a regular function in the class namespace, used for utility functions logically related to the class.

interview_qa.question: What is the purpose of 'self' in Python?
interview_qa.answer: 'self' represents the instance of the class. By using 'self', we can access the attributes and methods of the class in python. It binds the attributes with the given arguments. Python passes it automatically as the first argument when calling an instance method.

interview_qa.question: What is the MRO and how does Python resolve method calls in multiple inheritance?
interview_qa.answer: MRO (Method Resolution Order) determines which class's method is called. Python uses the C3 linearization algorithm: left-to-right depth-first with no duplicates. Check with `ClassName.__mro__`. super() follows the MRO, not necessarily the direct parent.

interview_qa.question: What is the difference between __str__ and __repr__?
interview_qa.answer: __str__ is for human-readable output (used by print/str()). __repr__ is for unambiguous developer representation — should ideally return code that recreates the object. If __str__ is not defined, Python falls back to __repr__. Always define __repr__ as minimum.

interview_qa.question: When does Python call __eq__ vs __hash__?
interview_qa.answer: If you override __eq__, Python sets __hash__ to None (making the object unhashable) unless you also define __hash__. Objects that compare equal must have the same hash. The contract: `a == b` implies `hash(a) == hash(b)`.

subtopics.title: @dataclass decorator and all parameters

subtopics.code:
from dataclasses import dataclass, field, fields, asdict, astuple, replace, make_dataclass, is_dataclass
from typing import ClassVar, List

@dataclass(order=True, frozen=False)
class Employee:
    # ClassVar - not included in __init__
    company: ClassVar[str] = 'Acme Corp'
    
    name: str
    department: str
    salary: float
    skills: List[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    _id: int = field(default=0, repr=False, compare=False)
    
    def __post_init__(self):
        if self.salary < 0:
            raise ValueError('Salary cannot be negative')
        self.name = self.name.strip().title()

e1 = Employee('alice', 'Engineering', 90000.0, ['Python', 'SQL'])
e2 = Employee('Bob', 'Marketing', 75000.0)

print(e1)
print(e1.company)
print(e1 > e2)           # order=True compares all fields
print(asdict(e1))
print(astuple(e1))
e3 = replace(e1, salary=95000.0)
print(e3)
print(fields(Employee))  # Field objects

subtopics.explanation: InitVar fields are passed to __init__ but not stored as attributes.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from dataclasses import InitVar

@dataclass
class DatabaseConnection:
    host: str
    port: int
    password: InitVar[str]  # passed to __post_init__ but not stored
    _connected: bool = field(default=False, init=False, repr=False)
    _conn_string: str = field(default='', init=False, repr=False)

db = DatabaseConnection('localhost', 5432, 'secret123')
print(db)
print(hasattr(db, 'password'))  # False!

interview_qa.question: Why use default_factory instead of a mutable default?
interview_qa.answer: Mutable defaults (like `field: list = []`) are shared across all instances — a classic Python bug. `default_factory=list` calls `list()` for each new instance, giving each its own list. This mirrors the mutable default argument trap.

interview_qa.question: When to use dataclass vs NamedTuple vs plain class?
interview_qa.answer: Use dataclass for mutable data with behavior, validation, and flexibility. Use NamedTuple for immutable records that need tuple-like unpacking. Use plain class for complex logic. Dataclasses are the modern default for simple data containers.

subtopics.title: try / except / else / finally
subtopics.explanation: try: code that might fail. except: handle errors. else: runs if no exception. finally: always runs.
subtopics.code:
def divide(a, b):
    try:
        result = a / b
    except ZeroDivisionError:
        print('Cannot divide by zero')
        return None
    except TypeError as e:
        print(f'Type error: {e}')
        return None
    else:
        print(f'Success: {result}')
        return result
    finally:
        print('divide() called')  # always runs

divide(10, 2)
print('---')
divide(10, 0)
print('---')
divide(10, 'a')

interview_qa.question: What is the difference between except Exception and except BaseException?
interview_qa.answer: except Exception catches all normal exceptions. except BaseException also catches SystemExit, KeyboardInterrupt, GeneratorExit — which you usually DON'T want to catch. Always use except Exception unless you have a specific reason to catch system-level events.

interview_qa.question: When does the else block in try/except run?
interview_qa.answer: The else block runs only if the try block completed without raising any exception. It's used to separate 'code that might fail' from 'code that should run on success', keeping the except block focused on actual error handling.

# batched (manual impl for < 3.12)

    while batch := tuple(islice(it, n)):

subtopics.title: groupby
subtopics.explanation: Group consecutive elements by a key. Input MUST be sorted by same key.
subtopics.code:
from itertools import groupby

# Must sort first!
data = [('Alice', 'Eng'), ('Bob', 'Eng'), ('Carol', 'HR'), ('Dave', 'HR'), ('Eve', 'Eng')]

# Sort by department
sorted_data = sorted(data, key=lambda x: x[1])

for dept, members in groupby(sorted_data, key=lambda x: x[1]):
    names = [m[0] for m in members]
    print(f'{dept}: {names}')

# Word frequency per first letter

for letter, group in groupby(words, key=lambda w: w[0]):
    print(f'{letter}: {list(group)}')

interview_qa.question: What is the most common mistake with groupby?
interview_qa.answer: Forgetting to sort the data first. groupby only groups CONSECUTIVE elements with the same key — if unsorted, you get multiple groups for the same key. Always: `sorted(data, key=keyfunc)` before `groupby(data, key=keyfunc)`.

interview_qa.question: How would you generate all subsets of a list?
interview_qa.answer: Using combinations across all lengths: `from itertools import combinations; subsets = [c for r in range(len(lst)+1) for c in combinations(lst, r)]`. This gives all 2^n subsets.

subtopics.title: lru_cache and cache
subtopics.explanation: Memoize function results to avoid recomputation.
subtopics.code:
from functools import lru_cache, cache
import time

# Without cache - recomputes every call
def fib_slow(n):
    if n < 2: return n
    return fib_slow(n-1) + fib_slow(n-2)

# With lru_cache
@lru_cache(maxsize=128)
def fib(n):
    if n < 2: return n
    return fib(n-1) + fib(n-2)

start = time.perf_counter()
print(fib(35))
print(f'Cached: {time.perf_counter()-start:.6f}s')

start = time.perf_counter()
print(fib_slow(35))
print(f'Uncached: {time.perf_counter()-start:.6f}s')

# Cache info
print(fib.cache_info())

# Clear cache
fib.cache_clear()
print(fib.cache_info())

# typed=True: f(3) and f(3.0) cached separately
@lru_cache(typed=True)
def typed_func(x):
    return type(x).__name__

print(typed_func(3))
print(typed_func(3.0))
print(typed_func.cache_info())

subtopics.title: total_ordering
subtopics.explanation: Fill in missing comparison methods automatically.
subtopics.code:
from functools import total_ordering

@total_ordering
class Version:
    def __init__(self, major, minor, patch):
        self.major = major
        self.minor = minor
        self.patch = patch
    
    def __eq__(self, other):
        return (self.major, self.minor, self.patch) == \
               (other.major, other.minor, other.patch)
    
    def __lt__(self, other):  # only need eq + one comparison
        return (self.major, self.minor, self.patch) < \
               (other.major, other.minor, other.patch)
    
    def __repr__(self):
        return f'v{self.major}.{self.minor}.{self.patch}'

v1 = Version(1, 0, 0)
v2 = Version(2, 0, 0)
v3 = Version(1, 5, 3)

print(v1 < v2)    # True
print(v1 > v2)    # False  (generated!)
print(v1 <= v3)   # True   (generated!)
print(sorted([v2, v1, v3]))

interview_qa.question: When would you use functools.partial vs a lambda?
interview_qa.answer: partial is better when: freezing named arguments for reuse, creating multiple variants of a function, when the result needs to be inspected (.func, .args, .keywords). Lambda is better for quick one-off transformations inline. partial also works correctly with pickle (lambdas don't).

interview_qa.question: What's the difference between lru_cache and cached_property?
interview_qa.answer: lru_cache caches based on function arguments — same args returns cached result. cached_property caches per instance with no arguments — computed once on first access, stored in instance __dict__. Use cached_property for expensive computed attributes on objects.

subtopics.title: Protocol (Duck Typing Formalized)
subtopics.explanation: Protocol defines an interface without requiring inheritance. If it has the right methods, it satisfies the protocol.
subtopics.code:
from typing import Protocol, runtime_checkable

@runtime_checkable
class Drawable(Protocol):
    def draw(self) -> str: ...
    def get_area(self) -> float: ...

class Circle:
    def __init__(self, r: float): self.r = r
    def draw(self) -> str: return f'Circle(r={self.r})'
    def get_area(self) -> float: return 3.14159 * self.r ** 2

class Square:
    def __init__(self, s: float): self.s = s
    def draw(self) -> str: return f'Square(s={self.s})'
    def get_area(self) -> float: return self.s ** 2

# Both satisfy Drawable without inheriting from it!
def render(shape: Drawable) -> None:
    print(f'{shape.draw()}, area={shape.get_area():.2f}')

render(Circle(5))
render(Square(4))

# runtime_checkable enables isinstance checks
print(isinstance(Circle(1), Drawable))   # True
print(isinstance('hello', Drawable))     # False

from typing import Literal, Final, ClassVar, Annotated
from dataclasses import dataclass

# Literal - only specific values allowed
Direction = Literal['north', 'south', 'east', 'west']

interview_qa.question: What is the difference between Protocol and ABC?
interview_qa.answer: ABC (Abstract Base Class) requires explicit inheritance: `class MyClass(MyABC)`. Protocol uses structural subtyping — if an object has the right methods/attributes, it satisfies the Protocol without inheritance. Protocol is the Pythonic way to formalize duck typing. ABCs are better for enforcing that subclasses implement required methods.

interview_qa.question: Does Python enforce type hints at runtime?
interview_qa.answer: No. Type hints are purely for static analysis tools (mypy, pyright) and IDE support. Python ignores them at runtime unless you use a library like pydantic or beartype. You can access annotations via __annotations__ or get_type_hints().

subtopics.title: All deque Methods
subtopics.explanation: deque provides O(1) appends and pops from both ends. Lists are O(n) for left operations.
subtopics.code:
from collections import deque

# Creation
d = deque([1, 2, 3, 4, 5])
print(d)

# append/appendleft
d.append(6)
d.appendleft(0)
print(d)

# pop/popleft
d.pop()       # removes 6
d.popleft()   # removes 0
print(d)

# extend/extendleft
d.extend([6, 7])
d.extendleft([-1, -2])  # note: each item appended left -> reversed!
print(d)

# rotate(n) - right rotation if n>0, left if n<0
d2 = deque([1,2,3,4,5])
d2.rotate(2)
print(d2)   # [4,5,1,2,3]
d2.rotate(-2)
print(d2)   # back to [1,2,3,4,5]

# maxlen - sliding window
window = deque(maxlen=3)
for i in range(6):
    window.append(i)
    print(list(window))

# Other methods
d3 = deque([1,2,3,2,1])
print(d3.count(2))         # 2
print(d3.index(3))         # 2
d3.insert(2, 99)
print(d3)
d3.remove(99)
d3.reverse()
print(d3)

interview_qa.question: When use deque over list?
interview_qa.answer: Use deque when you need O(1) appendleft/popleft (list is O(n) for these). Typical use cases: queues (FIFO), sliding windows with maxlen, breadth-first search, implementing undo history. For random access by index, list is still faster.

subtopics.title: OrderedDict Methods
subtopics.explanation: Dict with ordering-aware operations. Less needed since Python 3.7+ dicts preserve order.
subtopics.code:
from collections import OrderedDict

od = OrderedDict([('a', 1), ('b', 2), ('c', 3)])
print(od)

# move_to_end
od.move_to_end('a')           # move to end
print(od)
od.move_to_end('c', last=False)  # move to front
print(od)

# popitem(last=True) removes from end (LIFO)
key, val = od.popitem(last=True)
print(f'Removed: {key}={val}', od)

# OrderedDict equality checks ORDER
od1 = OrderedDict([('a',1),('b',2)])
od2 = OrderedDict([('b',2),('a',1)])
od3 = OrderedDict([('a',1),('b',2)])
print(od1 == od2)  # False! (different order)
print(od1 == od3)  # True
print(od1 == {'a':1,'b':2})  # True (regular dict ignores order)

subtopics.title: ChainMap — Layered Lookups
subtopics.explanation: Search through multiple dicts in order. Updates go to first map only.
subtopics.code:
from collections import ChainMap

# CLI args override env vars override defaults
defaults   = {'color': 'blue', 'user': 'guest', 'timeout': 30}
env_vars   = {'user': 'admin', 'timeout': 60}
cli_args   = {'color': 'red'}

cm = ChainMap(cli_args, env_vars, defaults)

print(cm['color'])    # red   (from cli_args)
print(cm['user'])     # admin (from env_vars)
print(cm['timeout'])  # 60    (from env_vars)

# Update only affects first map
cm['new_key'] = 'value'
print(cli_args)  # {'color':'red','new_key':'value'}

# new_child - create child scope
child = cm.new_child({'color': 'green'})
print(child['color'])  # green (from child)
print(cm['color'])     # red   (original unchanged)

# parents - all maps except first
print(list(cm.parents.maps))
print(list(cm.maps))

interview_qa.question: Why use UserDict instead of subclassing dict directly?
interview_qa.answer: When subclassing dict directly, some built-in methods (like update, __init__) don't call your overridden __setitem__, causing inconsistent behavior. UserDict stores data in self.data (a plain dict) and routes all operations through Python methods, so overrides work correctly every time.

subtopics.title: File & Directory Operations
subtopics.explanation: Core file system operations via the os module.
subtopics.code:
import os

# Current directory
print(os.getcwd())

# List directory
files = os.listdir('.')
print(files[:3])

# scandir - richer than listdir
for entry in os.scandir('.'):
    print(f'{entry.name}: isfile={entry.is_file()}')
    break  # just first entry

# os.walk - recursive tree traversal
for root, dirs, files in os.walk('/tmp', topdown=True):
    print(f'Dir: {root}')
    print(f'  Subdirs: {dirs}')
    print(f'  Files: {files}')
    break  # just root level

# Create / remove directories
os.makedirs('/tmp/test/nested', exist_ok=True)
print(os.path.exists('/tmp/test/nested'))  # True
os.removedirs('/tmp/test/nested')          # removes empty dirs

# File operations
with open('/tmp/test.txt', 'w') as f:
    f.write('hello')
os.rename('/tmp/test.txt', '/tmp/test2.txt')
print(os.path.exists('/tmp/test2.txt'))  # True
os.remove('/tmp/test2.txt')

interview_qa.answer: pathlib provides an OOP interface — paths are objects with methods, not strings. The / operator for joining is cleaner than os.path.join(). Methods like .read_text(), .write_text() simplify file I/O. Path objects work across OS. For new code, pathlib is always preferred.

subtopics.title: Thread Basics
subtopics.explanation: threading.Thread runs code concurrently. Best for I/O-bound tasks. GIL limits CPU-bound parallelism.
subtopics.code:
import threading
import time

def download(url, delay):
    print(f'Starting {url}')
    time.sleep(delay)  # simulate I/O
    print(f'Done {url}')

# Sequential
start = time.perf_counter()
download('site1.com', 1)
download('site2.com', 1)
print(f'Sequential: {time.perf_counter()-start:.1f}s')

# Threaded
start = time.perf_counter()
t1 = threading.Thread(target=download, args=('site1.com', 1))
t2 = threading.Thread(target=download, args=('site2.com', 1))
t1.start()
t2.start()
t1.join()  # wait for t1
t2.join()  # wait for t2
print(f'Threaded: {time.perf_counter()-start:.1f}s')

# Daemon thread - dies with main program
def background_task():
    while True:
        time.sleep(1)
        print('background tick')

daemon = threading.Thread(target=background_task, daemon=True)
daemon.start()
print('Main thread done')

subtopics.explanation: High-level interface for managing a pool of worker threads.
subtopics.code:
from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor, as_completed
import time

def fetch_data(url):
    time.sleep(0.5)  # simulate I/O
    return f'Data from {url}'

urls = [f'http://site{i}.com' for i in range(5)]

# map - submit all, collect in order
with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=3) as executor:
    results = list(executor.map(fetch_data, urls))
    print(results)

# submit + as_completed - get results as they finish
with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=3) as executor:
    futures = {executor.submit(fetch_data, url): url for url in urls}
    for future in as_completed(futures):
        url = futures[future]
        try:
            result = future.result()
            print(f'{url} -> {result}')
        except Exception as e:
            print(f'{url} failed: {e}')

interview_qa.question: What is the GIL and how does it affect threading?
interview_qa.answer: The Global Interpreter Lock (GIL) allows only one thread to execute Python bytecode at a time in CPython. This means CPU-bound threads don't actually run in parallel. However, the GIL is released during I/O operations, so threading is effective for I/O-bound tasks. For CPU-bound parallelism, use multiprocessing.

interview_qa.question: What is a race condition and how do you prevent it?
interview_qa.answer: A race condition occurs when multiple threads access and modify shared data concurrently, producing unpredictable results. Prevent with: Lock (mutex), RLock (reentrant lock), Semaphore (limit concurrent access), Queue (thread-safe communication), or using thread-local storage.

subtopics.title: Process and Pool
subtopics.explanation: multiprocessing bypasses the GIL for true CPU parallelism by using separate processes.
subtopics.code:
from multiprocessing import Process, Pool, Queue, Value, Array
import os

def cpu_work(n):
    # CPU-bound: sum of squares
    return sum(i*i for i in range(n))

# Pool.map - parallel map
if __name__ == '__main__':  # required guard on Windows/macOS
    with Pool(processes=4) as pool:
        results = pool.map(cpu_work, [10**6, 10**6, 10**6, 10**6])
        print(results[:2])
    
    # Pool.starmap - multiple args
    def add(a, b): return a + b
    with Pool(4) as pool:
        results = pool.starmap(add, [(1,2),(3,4),(5,6)])
        print(results)
    
    # Pool.apply_async - non-blocking
    with Pool(2) as pool:
        async_result = pool.apply_async(cpu_work, (10**5,))
        print('Submitted, doing other work...')
        print(async_result.get(timeout=10))

interview_qa.question: When use threading vs multiprocessing?
interview_qa.answer: Threading: I/O-bound tasks (network requests, file operations, DB queries) where threads wait most of the time. Multiprocessing: CPU-bound tasks (number crunching, image processing, ML) where you need true parallelism. Multiprocessing has higher overhead (process creation, IPC) but bypasses the GIL.

subtopics.title: async/await Basics
subtopics.explanation: Async programming uses a single thread with cooperative multitasking. Best for high-concurrency I/O.
subtopics.code:
import asyncio
import time

# Coroutine - defined with async def
async def fetch(url, delay):
    print(f'Fetching {url}')
    await asyncio.sleep(delay)  # non-blocking sleep
    return f'Response from {url}'

# Run coroutines sequentially
async def sequential():
    start = time.perf_counter()
    r1 = await fetch('site1.com', 1)
    r2 = await fetch('site2.com', 1)
    print(f'Sequential: {time.perf_counter()-start:.1f}s')
    return [r1, r2]

# Run concurrently with gather
async def concurrent():
    start = time.perf_counter()
    r1, r2, r3 = await asyncio.gather(
        fetch('site1.com', 1),
        fetch('site2.com', 1),
        fetch('site3.com', 1)
    )
    print(f'Concurrent: {time.perf_counter()-start:.1f}s')
    return [r1, r2, r3]

asyncio.run(sequential())
asyncio.run(concurrent())

subtopics.title: Tasks, gather, and asyncio patterns
subtopics.explanation: Create tasks for concurrent execution, handle timeouts and cancellation.
subtopics.code:
import asyncio

async def work(name, delay):
    print(f'{name} started')
    await asyncio.sleep(delay)
    print(f'{name} done')
    return name

async def main():
    # create_task - schedule immediately
    t1 = asyncio.create_task(work('Task1', 2))
    t2 = asyncio.create_task(work('Task2', 1))
    
    # await both (order of completion may differ)
    results = await asyncio.gather(t1, t2)
    print(f'Results: {results}')
    
    # Timeout
    try:
        await asyncio.wait_for(work('Slow', 5), timeout=1.0)
    except asyncio.TimeoutError:
        print('Timed out!')
    
    # as_completed - handle as each finishes
    tasks = [work(f'Job{i}', 3-i) for i in range(3)]
    for coro in asyncio.as_completed(tasks):
        result = await coro
        print(f'Finished: {result}')

asyncio.run(main())

subtopics.title: async for, async with, asyncio.Queue
subtopics.explanation: Async versions of iterators and context managers.
subtopics.code:
import asyncio

# Async context manager
class AsyncDBConnection:
    async def __aenter__(self):
        print('Connecting to DB...')
        await asyncio.sleep(0.1)  # simulate connection
        return self
    
    async def __aexit__(self, *args):
        print('Closing DB connection')
    
    async def query(self, sql):
        await asyncio.sleep(0.1)
        return f'Results for: {sql}'

# Async iterator
class AsyncRange:
    def __init__(self, n):
        self.n = n
        self.i = 0
    
    def __aiter__(self):
        return self
    
    async def __anext__(self):
        if self.i >= self.n:
            raise StopAsyncIteration
        await asyncio.sleep(0)  # yield control
        val = self.i
        self.i += 1
        return val

# asyncio.Queue - producer/consumer
async def producer(queue):
    for i in range(5):
        await queue.put(i)
        await asyncio.sleep(0.1)
    await queue.put(None)  # sentinel

async def consumer(queue):
    while True:
        item = await queue.get()
        if item is None: break
        print(f'Consumed: {item}')

async def main():
    async with AsyncDBConnection() as db:
        result = await db.query('SELECT *')
        print(result)
    
    async for i in AsyncRange(3):
        print(f'Async iter: {i}')
    
    queue = asyncio.Queue()
    await asyncio.gather(producer(queue), consumer(queue))

asyncio.run(main())

interview_qa.question: What is the difference between asyncio, threading, and multiprocessing?
interview_qa.answer: asyncio: single thread, cooperative multitasking via event loop — best for high-concurrency I/O (thousands of connections). Threading: multiple threads, preemptive — good for moderate I/O concurrency. Multiprocessing: multiple processes — only option for true CPU parallelism in CPython. asyncio has lowest overhead per concurrent unit.

interview_qa.question: What happens if you call a blocking function inside an async function?
interview_qa.answer: It blocks the entire event loop — no other coroutines can run. Always use async alternatives (aiohttp instead of requests, aiofiles instead of open). For unavoidable blocking calls, use `await loop.run_in_executor(None, blocking_func)` to run in a thread pool.

subtopics.title: Reference Counting and Garbage Collection
subtopics.explanation: CPython uses reference counting as primary memory management, with a cyclic garbage collector for cycles.
subtopics.code:
import sys
import gc

# Reference counting
a = [1, 2, 3]
print(sys.getrefcount(a))  # 2 (a + getrefcount arg)
b = a
print(sys.getrefcount(a))  # 3
del b
print(sys.getrefcount(a))  # 2

# Object size
print(sys.getsizeof([]))          # 56
print(sys.getsizeof([1,2,3]))     # 88
print(sys.getsizeof('hello'))     # 54
print(sys.getsizeof({}))          # 64

# Garbage collector (handles cycles)
class Node:
    def __init__(self):
        self.ref = None

a = Node()
b = Node()
a.ref = b  # a -> b
b.ref = a  # b -> a (cycle!)

del a, b
print(gc.collect())   # collects cyclic garbage

# GC settings
print(gc.get_threshold())  # (700, 10, 10) - generation thresholds
print(gc.get_count())      # objects in each generation

subtopics.title: __slots__ — Memory Optimization
subtopics.explanation: __slots__ replaces per-instance __dict__ with fixed-size array, reducing memory significantly.
subtopics.code:
import sys

# Normal class - uses __dict__
class PointDict:
    def __init__(self, x, y):
        self.x = x
        self.y = y

# Slots class - no __dict__
class PointSlots:
    __slots__ = ('x', 'y')
    def __init__(self, x, y):
        self.x = x
        self.y = y

d = PointDict(1, 2)
s = PointSlots(1, 2)

print(sys.getsizeof(d))          # ~48
print(sys.getsizeof(s))          # ~56 (smaller for many instances)
print(hasattr(d, '__dict__'))    # True
print(hasattr(s, '__dict__'))    # False

# Memory for 1M objects
import tracemalloc
tracemalloc.start()

points_dict  = [PointDict(i, i) for i in range(100000)]
current, peak = tracemalloc.get_traced_memory()
print(f'Dict: {peak/1024/1024:.1f} MB')
tracemalloc.reset_peak()

points_slots = [PointSlots(i, i) for i in range(100000)]
current, peak = tracemalloc.get_traced_memory()
print(f'Slots: {peak/1024/1024:.1f} MB')

interview_qa.question: How does Python manage memory?
interview_qa.answer: CPython uses reference counting: each object tracks how many references point to it. When count hits 0, memory is freed immediately. For cyclic references (A->B->A), a generational garbage collector runs periodically using gc module. Python also has a private heap and memory pools (pymalloc) for small objects.

interview_qa.question: When would you use __slots__?
interview_qa.answer: Use __slots__ when creating many instances of a class (millions) to reduce memory. It prevents dynamic attribute creation (no __dict__), reduces per-instance memory by ~30-50%, and slightly speeds up attribute access. Downside: cannot add arbitrary attributes, complicates multiple inheritance.

subtopics.title: type and Custom Metaclasses
subtopics.explanation: Classes are objects too. type is the metaclass of all classes. You can customize class creation.

interview_qa.question: When would you use a metaclass?
interview_qa.answer: Metaclasses are for 'class creation hooks': enforcing interface contracts, registering subclasses automatically (plugin systems), modifying class attributes at definition time, ORMs (like Django models). Most of the time, __init_subclass__, class decorators, or descriptors are simpler alternatives.

subtopics.title: Descriptor Protocol
subtopics.explanation: Descriptors implement __get__, __set__, __delete__ to customize attribute access. They power @property, @classmethod, @staticmethod.

interview_qa.question: What is the difference between a data descriptor and non-data descriptor?
interview_qa.answer: Data descriptor implements both __get__ and __set__ (or __delete__). Non-data descriptor implements only __get__. Data descriptors take priority over instance __dict__, so they always intercept access. Non-data descriptors are overridden by instance __dict__ entries. @property is a data descriptor; functions are non-data descriptors.

subtopics.title: Context Manager Protocol and contextlib
subtopics.explanation: __enter__ and __exit__ implement the with statement protocol.
subtopics.code:
import contextlib
import time

# Class-based context manager
class Timer:
    def __enter__(self):
        self.start = time.perf_counter()
        return self  # value bound to 'as' variable
    
    def __exit__(self, exc_type, exc_val, exc_tb):
        self.elapsed = time.perf_counter() - self.start
        print(f'Elapsed: {self.elapsed:.4f}s')
        return False  # don't suppress exceptions

with Timer() as t:
    sum(range(1000000))
print(f'Timer says: {t.elapsed:.4f}s')

# Generator-based with contextlib
@contextlib.contextmanager
def managed_resource(name):
    print(f'Acquiring {name}')
    resource = {'name': name, 'active': True}
    try:
        yield resource  # code in 'with' block runs here
    finally:
        resource['active'] = False
        print(f'Releasing {name}')

with managed_resource('DB Connection') as r:
    print(f'Using {r["name"]}')
print(f'Active: {r["active"]}')

# contextlib.suppress - swallow specific exceptions
with contextlib.suppress(FileNotFoundError):
    open('nonexistent.txt')
print('Continued after suppressed error')

# contextlib.nullcontext - conditional context manager
def process(use_timer=True):
    ctx = Timer() if use_timer else contextlib.nullcontext()
    with ctx:
        return sum(range(100000))

process(use_timer=True)

interview_qa.question: What should __exit__ return?
interview_qa.answer: Return True to suppress exceptions raised in the with block. Return False (or None) to propagate them. exc_type, exc_val, exc_tb are None if no exception occurred. The finally clause in @contextmanager always runs, making cleanup guaranteed.

subtopics.explanation: unittest is the built-in testing framework. Tests are methods starting with 'test_' inside TestCase subclasses.
subtopics.code:
import unittest

# Code under test
class Calculator:
    def add(self, a, b): return a + b
    def divide(self, a, b):
        if b == 0: raise ValueError('Cannot divide by zero')
        return a / b
    def is_even(self, n): return n % 2 == 0

class TestCalculator(unittest.TestCase):
    
    def setUp(self):  # runs before EACH test
        self.calc = Calculator()
    
    def tearDown(self):  # runs after EACH test
        pass
    
    @classmethod
    def setUpClass(cls):  # runs once before all tests
        print('\nStarting Calculator tests')
    
    def test_add(self):
        self.assertEqual(self.calc.add(2, 3), 5)
        self.assertEqual(self.calc.add(-1, 1), 0)
    
    def test_divide(self):
        self.assertAlmostEqual(self.calc.divide(10, 3), 3.333, places=3)
    
    def test_divide_by_zero(self):
        with self.assertRaises(ValueError) as ctx:
            self.calc.divide(5, 0)
        self.assertIn('zero', str(ctx.exception))
    
    def test_is_even(self):
        self.assertTrue(self.calc.is_even(4))
        self.assertFalse(self.calc.is_even(3))
    
    def test_all_assertions(self):
        # Equality
        self.assertEqual(1 + 1, 2)
        self.assertNotEqual(1, 2)
        # Identity
        self.assertIs(None, None)
        self.assertIsNone(None)
        self.assertIsNotNone(42)
        # Membership
        self.assertIn(3, [1,2,3])
        self.assertNotIn(5, [1,2,3])
        # Type
        self.assertIsInstance([], list)
        # Numeric
        self.assertGreater(5, 3)
        self.assertLess(2, 5)
        self.assertAlmostEqual(0.1+0.2, 0.3, places=10)
        # Collections
        self.assertListEqual([1,2,3], [1,2,3])
        self.assertDictEqual({'a':1}, {'a':1})

if __name__ == '__main__':
    unittest.main(verbosity=2)

print(m.method.called)              # True
print(m.method.call_count)          # 1
print(m.method.call_args)           # call(1, 2, key='value')

# Configure return value and side_effect
m2 = Mock()
m2.fetch.return_value = {'data': 42}
print(m2.fetch('url'))              # {'data': 42}

m3 = Mock()
m3.connect.side_effect = ConnectionError('Failed')
try:
    m3.connect()
except ConnectionError as e:
    print(e)

# MagicMock - supports magic methods
mm = MagicMock()
mm.__len__.return_value = 5
print(len(mm))  # 5

# patch - replace real objects in tests
class UserService:
    def get_user(self, user_id):
        import requests
        response = requests.get(f'https://api.example.com/users/{user_id}')
        return response.json()

# Test without real HTTP call
with patch('requests.get') as mock_get:
    mock_get.return_value.json.return_value = {'id': 1, 'name': 'Alice'}
    service = UserService()
    user = service.get_user(1)
    print(user)
    mock_get.assert_called_once_with('https://api.example.com/users/1')

# patch as decorator
@patch('requests.get')
def test_get_user(mock_get):
    mock_get.return_value.json.return_value = {'id': 1, 'name': 'Bob'}
    service = UserService()
    result = service.get_user(1)
    assert result['name'] == 'Bob'

test_get_user()

interview_qa.question: What is the difference between Mock and MagicMock?
interview_qa.answer: Mock is a basic mock object. MagicMock is a subclass that pre-configures all magic methods (__len__, __iter__, __str__, __enter__, __exit__, etc.) making it work with len(), for loops, with statements, etc. Use MagicMock when the code under test calls magic methods on the mock.

interview_qa.question: What is the difference between pytest fixtures and setUp/tearDown?
interview_qa.answer: pytest fixtures: composable (inject multiple), have scope (function/class/module/session), use yield for teardown, can be shared via conftest.py, and are more granular. setUp/tearDown run for every test in the class. pytest fixtures are more flexible and Pythonic.

subtopics.title: timeit, cProfile, line_profiler
subtopics.explanation: Measure where time is spent before optimizing.
subtopics.code:
import timeit
import cProfile
import pstats
import io

# timeit - micro-benchmarks
def list_append():
    lst = []
    for i in range(1000):
        lst.append(i)
    return lst

def list_comp():
    return [i for i in range(1000)]

# compare approaches
t1 = timeit.timeit(list_append, number=10000)
t2 = timeit.timeit(list_comp, number=10000)
print(f'append: {t1:.3f}s')
print(f'comp:   {t2:.3f}s')
print(f'comp is {t1/t2:.1f}x faster')

# timeit one-liner
result = timeit.timeit('[x**2 for x in range(100)]', number=100000)
print(f'{result:.3f}s')

# cProfile - full program profiling
def slow_function():
    total = 0
    for i in range(100000):
        total += i ** 2
    return total

pr = cProfile.Profile()
pr.enable()
result = slow_function()
pr.disable()

s = io.StringIO()
ps = pstats.Stats(pr, stream=s).sort_stats('cumulative')
ps.print_stats(5)
print(s.getvalue())

subtopics.title: Common Python Optimizations
subtopics.explanation: Key patterns for writing faster Python code.
subtopics.code:
import timeit
from functools import lru_cache

# 1. Local variable lookup is faster than global/attribute
def slow_loop(n):
    result = []
    append = result.append  # cache method lookup!
    for i in range(n):
        append(i)
    return result

# 2. Use built-ins (implemented in C)
nums = list(range(1000))

# Slow: Python loop
def py_sum(lst):
    total = 0
    for x in lst:
        total += x
    return total

# Fast: built-in
t1 = timeit.timeit(lambda: py_sum(nums), number=10000)
t2 = timeit.timeit(lambda: sum(nums), number=10000)
print(f'loop: {t1:.3f}s, sum(): {t2:.3f}s')

# 3. String join over concatenation
def bad_concat(parts):
    s = ''
    for p in parts:
        s += p  # O(n^2) - new object each time
    return s

def good_join(parts):
    return ''.join(parts)  # O(n)

parts = ['word'] * 1000
t1 = timeit.timeit(lambda: bad_concat(parts), number=1000)
t2 = timeit.timeit(lambda: good_join(parts), number=1000)
print(f'concat: {t1:.3f}s, join: {t2:.3f}s')

# 4. Memoization with lru_cache
@lru_cache(maxsize=None)
def fib(n):
    if n < 2: return n
    return fib(n-1) + fib(n-2)

print(fib(50))  # instant

# 5. Use sets for membership testing O(1) vs list O(n)
nums_list = list(range(10000))
nums_set  = set(range(10000))

t1 = timeit.timeit(lambda: 9999 in nums_list, number=100000)
t2 = timeit.timeit(lambda: 9999 in nums_set,  number=100000)
print(f'list: {t1:.3f}s, set: {t2:.3f}s')

interview_qa.question: What are the most impactful Python performance optimizations?
interview_qa.answer: 1) Use built-in functions (sum, map, filter) over loops — they're C-implemented. 2) Use sets for O(1) membership testing. 3) Use ''.join() not string concatenation. 4) Cache expensive results with lru_cache. 5) Use generators for large data. 6) Profile first — never guess. 7) For numeric heavy-lifting, use NumPy vectorization.

interview_qa.question: How do Python's first-class functions affect design patterns?
interview_qa.answer: Many Gang of Four patterns become simpler in Python. Strategy pattern: pass a function instead of a strategy object. Command pattern: use callables. Observer: pass callbacks. Factory: use dict of classes. Python's first-class functions, closures, and decorators replace much boilerplate that OOP patterns require in Java/C++.

interview_qa.question: What is EAFP vs LBYL in Python?
interview_qa.answer: EAFP (Easier to Ask Forgiveness than Permission): try/except — Pythonic. `try: x = d['key'] except KeyError: ...`. LBYL (Look Before You Leap): check before acting — `if 'key' in d: x = d['key']`. EAFP is generally preferred in Python since it avoids race conditions and is more readable for common cases.

subtopics.title: requests and httpx
subtopics.explanation: Making HTTP requests with proper error handling, retries, and timeouts.
subtopics.code:
import requests
from requests.adapters import HTTPAdapter
from requests.packages.urllib3.util.retry import Retry

# Basic GET
response = requests.get(
    'https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/posts/1',
    timeout=5
)
print(response.status_code)   # 200
print(response.json()['title'])

# POST with JSON body
response = requests.post(
    'https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/posts',
    json={'title': 'Test', 'body': 'Content', 'userId': 1},
    headers={'Content-Type': 'application/json'}
)
print(response.status_code)   # 201

# Proper error handling
def safe_get(url, **kwargs):
    try:
        response = requests.get(url, timeout=10, **kwargs)
        response.raise_for_status()  # raises for 4xx/5xx
        return response.json()
    except requests.exceptions.Timeout:
        print('Request timed out')
    except requests.exceptions.HTTPError as e:
        print(f'HTTP error: {e.response.status_code}')
    except requests.exceptions.ConnectionError:
        print('Connection failed')
    return None

# Session with retry logic
session = requests.Session()
retry = Retry(total=3, backoff_factor=1, status_forcelist=[429,500,502,503,504])
session.mount('https://', HTTPAdapter(max_retries=retry))

# Async with httpx
import asyncio
import httpx

async def fetch_all(urls):
    async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=10) as client:
        tasks = [client.get(url) for url in urls]
        responses = await asyncio.gather(*tasks)
        return [r.json() for r in responses]

urls = ['https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/posts/1',
        'https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/posts/2']
results = asyncio.run(fetch_all(urls))
print([r['id'] for r in results])

subtopics.title: sqlite3 and SQLAlchemy
subtopics.explanation: Database access using built-in sqlite3 and SQLAlchemy ORM.
subtopics.code:
import sqlite3
from contextlib import contextmanager

# sqlite3 - built-in
@contextmanager
def get_db(path=':memory:'):
    conn = sqlite3.connect(path)
    conn.row_factory = sqlite3.Row  # access by column name
    try:
        yield conn
        conn.commit()
    except Exception:
        conn.rollback()
        raise
    finally:
        conn.close()

with get_db() as db:
    # Create table
    db.execute('''CREATE TABLE users (
        id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
        name TEXT NOT NULL,
        email TEXT UNIQUE,
        age INTEGER
    )''')
    
    # Insert with parameterized queries (prevent SQL injection)
    db.execute('INSERT INTO users (name, email, age) VALUES (?,?,?)',
               ('Alice', 'alice@example.com', 30))
    db.executemany('INSERT INTO users (name, email, age) VALUES (?,?,?)',
                   [('Bob', 'bob@example.com', 25),
                    ('Carol', 'carol@example.com', 35)])
    
    # Query
    cursor = db.execute('SELECT * FROM users WHERE age > ?', (24,))
    for row in cursor:
        print(dict(row))
    
    # Aggregate
    result = db.execute('SELECT AVG(age) as avg_age FROM users').fetchone()
    print(f'Avg age: {result["avg_age"]:.1f}')

interview_qa.question: What is SQL injection and how do you prevent it?
interview_qa.answer: SQL injection is when user input is embedded directly in SQL strings, allowing attackers to modify queries. Prevent by ALWAYS using parameterized queries: `cursor.execute('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?', (user_id,))` — NEVER f-strings or string concatenation for SQL.

import csv, json, pickle
from io import StringIO

# CSV reading and writing

config = {
    'host': 'localhost',

    'options': ['ssl', 'timeout'],

}

# Serialize
json_str = json.dumps(config, indent=2)

# Deserialize
parsed = json.loads(json_str)
print(parsed['options'])

# Custom encoder for non-serializable types
from datetime import datetime

class DateTimeEncoder(json.JSONEncoder):
    def default(self, obj):
        if isinstance(obj, datetime):
            return obj.isoformat()
        return super().default(obj)

data_with_date = {'created': datetime.now(), 'value': 42}
print(json.dumps(data_with_date, cls=DateTimeEncoder))

part18_interview_master.title: Interview Master: Top 50 Python Interview Q&A

questions.question: What is the difference between list, tuple, set, and dict?
questions.answer: list: ordered, mutable, allows duplicates, O(n) search. tuple: ordered, immutable, hashable, slightly faster. set: unordered, mutable, unique elements, O(1) search/add/remove. dict: key-value pairs, ordered (3.7+), O(1) key access. Use: list for sequences, tuple for fixed records, set for unique membership, dict for lookups.

questions.question: Explain Python's memory management.
questions.answer: CPython uses reference counting: each object has a refcount. When it hits 0, memory is freed. A cyclic garbage collector (gc module) handles reference cycles. Python has a private heap managed by pymalloc for small objects. __slots__ reduces per-instance memory. del decrements refcount; sys.getrefcount() inspects it.

questions.question: What is a decorator? Write one from scratch.
questions.answer: A decorator is a callable that takes a function and returns a modified function. Example: `def log(func): @wraps(func) def wrapper(*a,**kw): print(f'Calling {func.__name__}'); return func(*a,**kw); return wrapper`. Always use @functools.wraps to preserve __name__, __doc__.

questions.question: What is the difference between shallow and deep copy?
questions.answer: Shallow copy (copy.copy / .copy() / [:]) copies the container but nested objects are still shared references. Deep copy (copy.deepcopy) recursively copies all nested objects. Example: list of lists — modifying a nested list in a shallow copy affects the original.

questions.question: Explain generators and when to use them.
questions.answer: Generators are functions that yield values lazily. They use O(1) memory regardless of sequence size. Use when: processing large/infinite sequences, building data pipelines, values only needed once. Created with yield keyword or generator expressions (x for x in iterable).

questions.question: What is the difference between @staticmethod and @classmethod?
questions.answer: @staticmethod receives no implicit first argument — it's a regular function in the class namespace. @classmethod receives cls (the class itself) as first argument. Use @classmethod for factory methods (alternate constructors), access to class state. Use @staticmethod for utility functions logically grouped with the class.

questions.question: How does Python resolve multiple inheritance (MRO)?
questions.answer: Python uses C3 linearization (MRO). For class C(A, B): Python builds a list that preserves left-to-right order and ensures a class appears before its parents. Check with Class.__mro__. super() follows MRO, not just the direct parent.

questions.question: What are *args and **kwargs?
questions.answer: *args captures extra positional args as a tuple. **kwargs captures extra keyword args as a dict. In a function signature, order must be: positional, *args, keyword-only, **kwargs. They can also unpack when calling: func(*list, **dict).

questions.question: Explain the difference between is and ==.
questions.answer: == checks value equality (__eq__). is checks identity (same object in memory, same id()). Small integers (-5 to 256) and interned strings may share identity. Never use 'is' to compare values — always use == for equality, is only for None checks (x is None).

questions.question: What is a context manager and how do you create one?
questions.answer: A context manager wraps setup/teardown around a with block. Implement __enter__ (returns resource) and __exit__ (cleanup, suppress exception if returns True). Or use @contextlib.contextmanager with a generator (yield is the body of the with block).

questions.question: What is the GIL and when does it matter?
questions.answer: Global Interpreter Lock — CPython mutex allowing only one thread to run Python bytecode at once. Released during I/O operations. Matters for CPU-bound multithreaded code — threads don't parallelize. Solutions: multiprocessing (separate processes), asyncio (async I/O), or use C extensions that release the GIL (e.g. NumPy).

questions.question: How do you handle exceptions properly?
questions.answer: Catch specific exceptions (not bare except). Use try/except/else/finally correctly. Re-raise with `raise` or chain with `raise X from Y`. Create custom exceptions for domain errors. Never swallow exceptions silently. Log with logger.exception() to preserve traceback.

questions.question: What is the difference between append() and extend()?
questions.answer: append(x) adds x as a single element (even if x is a list — you get a nested list). extend(iterable) adds each element of the iterable individually. [1,2].append([3,4]) → [1,2,[3,4]]. [1,2].extend([3,4]) → [1,2,3,4].

questions.question: Explain list comprehension vs generator expression vs map().
questions.answer: List comp: creates full list in memory, use when you need to reuse or index the result. Generator: lazy, one item at a time, O(1) memory, use for single-pass iteration. map(): applies function lazily, like a generator. For readability, list/gen comprehensions are preferred. map() is fast for simple built-in functions.

questions.question: What is functools.lru_cache? When would you use it?
questions.answer: lru_cache memoizes a function's results based on arguments. Subsequent calls with same args return cached result. Use for: pure functions called repeatedly with same inputs, expensive computations (Fibonacci, DB lookups in read-only scenarios). Set maxsize=None for unlimited cache. Use cache() as shorthand in Python 3.9+.

questions.question: How would you find duplicates in a list?
questions.answer: Using Counter: `[x for x, c in Counter(lst).items() if c > 1]`. Using sets: `seen = set(); dups = set(x for x in lst if x in seen or seen.add(x))`. For just checking existence: `len(lst) != len(set(lst))`.

questions.question: What is the difference between module and package?
questions.answer: A module is a single .py file. A package is a directory containing an __init__.py (makes it a package) and possibly submodules/subpackages. Import with `import module` or `from package.module import name`.

questions.question: Explain Python's LEGB scope rule.
questions.answer: Python resolves names in this order: Local (inside current function), Enclosing (outer function scopes), Global (module level), Built-in (Python builtins). Use `global` to assign to global scope, `nonlocal` to assign to enclosing function scope.

questions.question: What is a metaclass?
questions.answer: A metaclass is the class of a class — it controls how classes are created. type is the default metaclass. Custom metaclasses inherit from type and override __new__ or __init__. Used for: ORM models, singleton enforcement, automatic method registration. Prefer __init_subclass__ or class decorators for simpler cases.

questions.question: How do you make a class iterable?
questions.answer: Implement __iter__ (returns iterator) and __next__ (returns next value, raises StopIteration when done). Or implement __iter__ as a generator function (uses yield). Or __getitem__ with integer indices (legacy protocol).

questions.question: What is the difference between sorted() and list.sort()?
questions.answer: sorted() works on any iterable, returns a new list, original unchanged. list.sort() modifies in-place, returns None, only works on lists. Both accept key= and reverse= parameters. list.sort() is slightly faster (no copy).

questions.question: How do you reverse a list? What are the different approaches?
questions.answer: 1) lst[::-1] — creates new reversed list. 2) list(reversed(lst)) — lazy reversed() iterator. 3) lst.reverse() — in-place, returns None. For strings: s[::-1]. For generators: can't reverse directly, convert to list first.

questions.question: Explain Python's data model (dunder methods).
questions.answer: Python's data model lets custom classes integrate with language features via __dunder__ methods. __len__ enables len(), __iter__ enables for loops, __add__ enables +, __enter__/__exit__ enables with. This is why Python's built-in functions and operators work on custom objects — they call these protocols.

questions.question: What is the walrus operator (:=)?
questions.answer: The walrus operator (Python 3.8+) assigns and returns a value in a single expression. Useful in while loops: `while chunk := f.read(8192): process(chunk)` or comprehensions: `[y for x in data if (y := f(x)) > 0]`. Avoids calling the same function twice.

questions.question: How do you handle circular imports in Python?
questions.answer: Options: 1) Restructure code to remove cycle. 2) Move import inside the function that needs it (lazy import). 3) Import the module, not the name: `import module; module.name`. 4) Use TYPE_CHECKING guard for type-hint-only imports. Root cause is usually poor module structure.
